calendar.matters:
Motion For New Trial
Off Calendar Dec-30-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Geoffrey Rotwein, Esq. 415-397-0860 for Petitioner.
John McGlothlin, Esq. 510-879-0295 of the Office of the Attorney General for Respondent.

RULING - A R G U E D. Petitioner argues. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PETITIONER PATRICK CORKERY's Motion For New Trial.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Petitioner's motion for new trial is denied. Petitioner fails to demonstrate that the court's "decision is against [the] law." (Code Civ. Proc., 657(6).)

Petitioner improperly raised the argument regarding the timeliness of respondent's answer in his reply brief. As the court previously noted, petitioner had the information that it needed to assert this issue prior to filing his moving papers, but he failed to do so. The reply brief was not rebutting an argument that respondent raised. Petitioner therefore waived the matter. Petitioner's citation to and reliance on an unpublished Court of Appeal decision is improper. (Cal. R. Ct. 8.1115(a).) The court rejects Petitioner's res judicata argument, which is ill-founded.

The court did not err by relying on the unsworn police report in rendering its decision. The DS 367 and the unsworn report both stated that the admonition was provided. The unsworn report could supplement the sworn DS 367. (MacDonald v. Gutierrez (2004) 32 Cal.4th 150, 159 ["the Legislature clearly anticipates the sworn report will contain all or nearly all of the information necessary to remove the offender's license. In light of this legislative intent, the sworn report cannot be wholly devoid of relevant information. However, so long as a sworn report is filed, it is consistent with the relaxed evidentiary standards of an administrative per se hearing that technical omissions of proof can be corrected by an unsworn report filed by the arresting officer."].) Respondent made a prima facie showing that the admonition was provided and petitioner provided no contrary evidence. It was therefore proper for the court to deny the petition. (See Murphey v. Shimoto (2017) 13 Cal.App.5th 1052, 1070 [after department makes prima facie showing, burden shifts to driver to dispute the showing].) (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Grant Petition For Writ Of Administrative Mandamus
Off Calendar Oct-03-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Geoffrey Rotwein, Esq. 415-397-0860 for Petitioner.
John McGlothlin, Deputy Attorney General, 510-879-0295, Office of the Attorney General for Respondent DMV.

RULING - A R G U E D. Petitioner cites a Supreme Court Case, vehicle code section. Respondent argues. The Court shares its views re: cases.(as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PETITIONER PATRICK CORKERY's Motion To Grant Petition For Writ Of Administrative Mandamus.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Petitioner Corkery's petition for writ of administrative mandamus is denied.

The court rejects petitioner's timeliness argument. It appears that respondent received the complete record by August 6, 2019, but the date of receipt is not entirely clear. (AR, 64.) Petitioner argues that respondent's answer was filed more than 30 days later, was untimely under Code of Civ. Proc. 1089.5, and petitioner's allegations are therefore deemed admitted.

The court rejects this argument. First, the argument was made for the first time in the reply papers and there was nothing that prevented petitioner from raising this issue in his initial moving papers. (See Hernandez v. First Student, Inc. (2019) 37 Cal.App.4th 270, 277 ["[w]e will not ordinarily consider issues raised for the first time in a reply brief."]; American Drug Stores, Inc. v. Stroh (1992) 10 Cal.App.4th 1446, 1453 ["Points raised for the first time in a reply brief will ordinarily not be considered, because such consideration would deprive the respondent of an opportunity to counter the argument."].) The court notes that respondent's answer was filed on September 9, 2019, and that was the same date the petitioner filed his notice of motion. Petitioner was in possession of AR 64 when he filed his moving papers, but failed to raise the timeliness issue. Second, petitioner's authorities stand for the proposition that the allegations of the petition are taken as true where no answer is filed, not where an untimely answer is filed. (See Block v. Superior Court (1998) 62 Cal.App.4th 363, 368, fn. 2 ["Failure to answer the petition is considered an admission of the truth of the allegations."]

Exercising its independent judgment, the court concludes that the weight of the evidence supports respondent's decision. The hearing officer reasonably concluded that the chemical admonition was provided, and petitioner refused to comply even though the DS-367 and collision report are not entirely consistent. The DS-367 indicates that Officer Hoang provided the admonition. The collision report indicates that Officer Tsang provided the admonition (AR 20). The court concludes that Officer Tsang provided the admonition at the station and Hoang was present to observe it since both Tsang and Hoang transported petitioner to the police station. (AR 20.) Hoang's shifting statements were the result of sloppy paperwork, not perjury.

Administrative hearings are not conducted with the same strictness as a court proceeding. (See Gov't. Code, 11513(c) ["The hearing need not be conducted according to technical rules relating to evidence"]; MacDonald v. Gutierrez (2004) 32 Cal.4th 150, 153 and 159 [hearing officer can consider unsworn officer statement]; Lake v. Reed (1997) 16 Cal.4th 448, 461 ["A police officer's report, even if unsworn, constitutes 'the sort of evidence on which responsible persons are accustomed to rely in the conduct of serious affairs.' "].)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
